analysis: Across the stronger heuristics I see the same high‑value building blocks: deterministic Pareto prefiltering, cheap per‑point proxies (box volume and small-weight scalarizations) to rank candidates, a compact k‑proportional shortlist, cached singleton HVs, a CELF (lazy greedy) selection to minimize exact HV recomputations, and a limited 1‑for‑1 local swap cleanup. Best designs balance shortlist size, caching (singletons and selective pairwise only when small), and HV‑call budgets. Weaker variants either (a) make too many exact HV calls (rebuilding arrays with np.vstack in inner loops), (b) use inefficient Python membership/list ops (e.g. "if cand in selected_idx", list.remove) causing O(N) checks inside loops, or (c) overcompute nondominance naively without vectorized masks or early exits. Several heuristics correctly add upper‑bound pruning (box_vol or singleton HV) and deterministic tie‑breakers; others miss effective pruning or reuse of precomputed diffs, causing needless work. Good tradeoffs: invest a bit in quality proxies (weight vectors or G/HVC approximations) to drastically shrink the candidate set, then use rigorous lazy greedy + small local search under a hard HV budget.
experience: Make next‑gen heuristics: (1) rank by cheap proxies to form an adaptive shortlist, (2) cache singleton HVs and use CELF lazy updates, (3) prune with box‑volume/singleton upper bounds and cap HV calls, (4) avoid Python O(N) membership in inner loops (use sets/indices), and (5) only pairwise‑cache when shortlist ≲40. Keep shortlist size adaptive to k and Pareto size.
current_reflection: Make next‑gen heuristics: (1) rank by cheap proxies to form an adaptive shortlist, (2) cache singleton HVs and use CELF lazy updates, (3) prune with box‑volume/singleton upper bounds and cap HV calls, (4) avoid Python O(N) membership in inner loops (use sets/indices), and (5) only pairwise‑cache when shortlist ≲40. Keep shortlist size adaptive to k and Pareto size.
good_reflections: Prefilter dominated points, approximate HVC with few weight vectors to shortlist candidates, then perform a local search using cached incremental HVC deltas. Minimize Python-side HV calls by batching and avoid nested HV recomputations. Make the algorithm deterministic (seeded) and ensure it always returns exactly k points.
bad_reflections: Make shortlists adaptive (upper-bound marginal gain → size), use CELF with cached singleton (and selective pairwise) HVs, and prune candidates by cheap upper bounds before exact HV. Implement incremental HV updates or fast specialized HV routines, batch/parallel HV evaluations, deterministic tie-breaks, and small bounded local swap budgets. Diversify a few scalarization weights, but avoid huge weight sets unless approximation budgets are explicit.

Prioritize (1) a strong deterministic shortlist: blend normalized rect_vol and scalarized scores, (2) precompute singleton HVs and selective pairwise HVs, (3) use CELF lazy greedy with timestamping and cheap upper‑bound pruning, (4) limit swaps to top candidates and update sets with O(1) structures, and (5) replace Python O(N^2) dominance checks / hot loops with vectorized or compiled routines and parallel HV evaluations.

Prefer a compact k‑proportional shortlist scored by vectorized box+multiweight scalarization, cache singleton HVs, run CELF lazy greedy with cheap upper‑bound pruning, then do limited deterministic 1‑for‑1 swaps. Vectorize nondominated filtering, avoid nested Python loops, only pairwise-cache when shortlist is small, and instrument an HV‑call budget. This yields strong hypervolume with predictable runtime.
comprehensive_reflection:
- Keywords: adaptive shortlist, CELF, singleton HV cache, box‑volume upper bounds, pairwise cache ≤40, vectorize, deterministic, HV budget
- Advice: Build an adaptive shortlist (function of k and Pareto size) using cheap proxies (normalized box vol + few scalarizations); precompute singleton HVs, run CELF lazy greedy with cached deltas/timestamps; batch and cap HV calls; follow with limited deterministic local swaps.
- Avoid: Python O(N) membership/inner loops, nested exact HV recomputations, unconditional pairwise caching for large shortlists, nondeterministic tie‑breaks.
- Explanation: Cuts exact HV calls and memory, yields deterministic exact‑k outputs, and trades small bounded local search for large runtime and quality gains.
